title: Early Quran Manuscripts: What the Physical Evidence Shows
subtitle: The Birmingham fragment, the Sanaa palimpsest, and the Samarkand and Topkapi codices, and what they do and do not prove
summary: This article surveys the most important early Quran manuscripts, including the radiocarbon-dated Birmingham fragment, the debated Sanaa palimpsest, and the Samarkand and Topkapi codices. It explains honestly what each manuscript establishes and what it does not, and compares this record fairly to the manuscript history of the Bible, including Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus. The conclusion separates the historical question of early copying from the theological claim of the Quran's own preservation.

seo.metaTitle: Early Quran Manuscripts: Birmingham, Sanaa, Samarkand Explained
seo.metaDescription: An honest survey of the oldest surviving Quran manuscripts, the Birmingham fragment, the Sanaa palimpsest debate, and the Samarkand and Topkapi codices, compared fairly to the Bible's earliest manuscripts.

sections.title: The Short Answer
sections.body:
Manuscripts are physical, datable objects: pieces of parchment or paper with ink on them, sitting today in libraries and museums. When historians study old copies of the Quran, they are not asking what Muslims believe about the Quran's origin. They are asking a narrower, answerable question: how old is this particular physical page, and what text does it contain?

This article walks through the most important surviving early Quran manuscripts: a fragment held at the University of Birmingham, a unique double-layered manuscript found in Sanaa, Yemen, and two famous codices kept in Tashkent and Istanbul. It explains honestly what each one does and does not prove, including a genuine scholarly debate over the Sanaa manuscript that this article will not paper over. The goal is to see clearly what the physical evidence actually shows, alongside how Muslims understand the Quran's preservation as a matter of faith.

sections.title: How Manuscripts Get Dated
sections.body:
Two main scientific methods let historians estimate the age of an old manuscript. Radiocarbon dating measures the decay of a radioactive form of carbon in organic material, in this case the parchment (prepared animal skin) itself, and produces a date range, usually stated with a confidence level such as 95.4 percent. Paleography works differently: it dates a manuscript by comparing its handwriting style, letter shapes, and page layout to other manuscripts whose dates are already known some other way.

Both methods have limits worth naming honestly. Radiocarbon dating tells you when the animal whose skin became the parchment died, not necessarily the exact year the ink was applied. In practice, prepared parchment was expensive and was normally written on within a reasonably short time afterward, so the two dates are usually close, but they are not identical by definition. Paleography depends on having enough comparison manuscripts, and dating estimates can shift as scholars debate how quickly early Arabic script styles changed. With those honest limits in view, here is what the surviving physical evidence for the Quran's early text actually looks like.

sections.title: The Question This Article Answers
sections.body:
The question here is narrow and answerable: what do the oldest surviving physical copies of the Quran actually show about how early, and how consistently, its text was written down? That is different from asking whether the Quran is divinely revealed, which is a question of faith rather than radiocarbon labs. It is a historical question about ink, parchment, and dates.

The same kind of question can be asked of the Bible, whose own manuscript history is well documented by Christian scholarship itself. Comparing the two records fairly, without inflating either one, is the point of this article. Muslims are sometimes told that no early manuscripts of the Quran survive at all, and are sometimes told the opposite exaggeration, that a single manuscript discovery like the Sanaa palimpsest overturns the standard text. Neither claim survives contact with what specialists in the field actually argue.

sections.title: Where These Manuscripts Were Found
sections.body:
Several manuscript discoveries anchor this discussion. In 1972, workers restoring the roof space of the Great Mosque of Sanaa in Yemen found a large cache of damaged parchment pages that had been stored away rather than destroyed, some of them apparently for over a thousand years. Among them was a manuscript now known as the Sanaa palimpsest: a parchment that had been written on, then washed to remove the ink, and written on again with a different text, a common and economical practice when parchment was costly. Modern imaging techniques, including ultraviolet light, can recover much of the erased lower text beneath the later upper text.

A different kind of discovery happened at the University of Birmingham. Two leaves of parchment had been sitting for decades in the Mingana Collection of Middle Eastern manuscripts, held at the university's Cadbury Research Library, misbound together with a separate and later seventh-century Quran manuscript. Researchers eventually separated the two leaves and sent the older ones for radiocarbon testing at the University of Oxford's Radiocarbon Accelerator Unit.

Two other famous copies, often shown to visitors and pictured in books about Islamic history, are the large codices kept today in Tashkent, Uzbekistan (popularly called the Samarkand codex, after the city where it was long housed) and in the Topkapi Palace Museum in Istanbul. Both are traditionally associated with Caliph Uthman, the third successor to Muhammad, who Islamic historical accounts credit with standardizing the Quran's text into a single agreed form around the 650s CE and sending copies to the major cities of the growing Muslim community. Specialists who study the handwriting, and in the Samarkand codex's case the parchment itself, of these two codices generally judge them to be genuinely old and significant witnesses to the early text, while cautioning that the popular claim that either one is literally Uthman's own personal copy goes beyond what the physical evidence can confirm.

sections.title: The Quran's Own Claim: A Preserved Tablet
sections.body:
The Quran makes a theological claim about its own preservation that stands apart from any manuscript. Surah Al-Buruj states: "But this is an honored Qur'an, [Inscribed] in a Preserved Slate" (Quran 85:21-22). Muslim scholars have long read these verses as describing a heavenly original, a guarded and changeless template, rather than making a claim about any single earthly copy of ink and parchment. This is a claim of faith, and it is worth being honest that no radiocarbon test or paleographic study can confirm or disconfirm a claim about a heavenly tablet. Physical evidence and theological doctrine are answering different questions.

What manuscripts can speak to is a related but separate question: was the earthly, human process of copying the Quran early, careful, and consistent? Here the physical evidence is unusually strong for a seventh-century religious text. The Birmingham leaves, radiocarbon dated by Oxford to a range of AD 568 to 645 with 95.4 percent confidence, contain material from Surahs 18 to 20 that reads essentially as the same text found in printed Qurans today. Professor David Thomas of the University of Birmingham observed that the parchment's animal was alive during Muhammad's lifetime or shortly afterward, meaning the writing on it can, with reasonable confidence, be dated to within roughly two decades of his death. For Muslims, this kind of early and stable written witness functions as historical corroboration alongside the doctrine of the Preserved Tablet, not a substitute for faith in it, but consistent with it.

sections.title: For Comparison: The Bible's Earliest Complete Manuscripts
sections.body:
Christian scripture does not make an equivalent claim about a single preserved heavenly tablet, and mainstream Christian scholarship approaches the Bible's textual history differently, through the discipline of textual criticism. Textual criticism openly compares thousands of surviving manuscripts, notes where they differ from one another, and works to reconstruct the most probable original wording. This is a respected, mainstream tool within biblical scholarship itself, developed and practiced largely by Christian scholars, not an outside attack on the tradition.

The earliest manuscripts containing a complete Bible are later, relative to their founding events, than the Birmingham Quran fragment. Codex Sinaiticus, held today mainly at the British Library, was written in the middle of the fourth century and is described by the manuscript's own custodians as containing "the earliest complete copy of the Christian New Testament," a text finished roughly three centuries after the events it describes. Codex Vaticanus, kept in the Vatican Library, dates from around the same fourth-century period. Both manuscripts show clear evidence of the ordinary process of hand-copying over generations: Codex Sinaiticus itself has been, in the words of the project that curates it, "heavily annotated by a series of early correctors," a normal sign of a long and living manuscript tradition rather than a flaw unique to Christian scripture.

sections.title: Comparing the Two Manuscript Records
sections.body:
Placed side by side, the two manuscript records differ mainly in how soon after their founding events a complete or near-complete physical copy survives. The Birmingham leaves may sit within a couple of decades of Muhammad's death in 632 CE, and the Sanaa palimpsest's lower text is also generally placed within the first century of Islam. By contrast, the earliest surviving complete Bible manuscripts, Sinaiticus and Vaticanus, were copied roughly three centuries after the events described in the Gospels.

This comparison should be stated fairly rather than triumphantly. A shorter gap between an event and its surviving physical manuscripts is not automatic proof of a cleaner transmission; it is one piece of evidence among several. The New Testament's own textual history also includes earlier fragmentary papyri that are not complete Bibles but do predate Sinaiticus by generations. Likewise, both religious traditions relied heavily on oral transmission before and alongside writing: early Christian communities preserved and copied letters and Gospel accounts for decades before Sinaiticus was produced, just as the Quran was memorized by companions of Muhammad both before and after it was written down.

What can be said honestly is this: the manuscript record for the Quran's text, taken as a whole, shows an unusually short gap between the claimed date of revelation and the earliest surviving written witnesses, a gap that mainstream biblical scholarship, again speaking in its own terms, does not claim for a complete New Testament manuscript.

sections.title: Common Misconceptions, In Both Directions
sections.body:
A first misconception moves too fast in the Quran's favor: the claim that the Birmingham leaves alone "prove" the entire modern Quran, unchanged word for word, already existed in the 630s. The Birmingham fragment is two leaves covering parts of three surahs, 18 through 20, out of 114 in the whole Quran. It is genuine and important evidence for an early, stable text of those specific passages. It is not, by itself, evidence about the whole book's history.

A second misconception moves too fast in the other direction: the claim that the Sanaa palimpsest's lower text "proves" the Quran was substantially rewritten or corrupted. This deserves care, because it is a genuinely debated case rather than a settled one in either direction. The lower text does contain real differences from the standard Uthmanic text, including variations in wording and, in places, a different ordering of surahs. Scholars who have studied it closely, including Behnam Sadeghi and Mohsen Goudarzi, have argued that these variants place the lower text within the same broad textual family as the standard Quran, closely comparable to Islamic historical accounts describing Companions of Muhammad, such as Ibn Mas'ud, who kept personal codices with different verse orderings and occasional wording differences before Caliph Uthman standardized a single text for the whole community. Other specialists, such as Asma Hilali, have proposed different explanations for the manuscript's origin and purpose, and the scholarly conversation about how best to characterize it continues. What the debate is not about, on any side taken seriously in the field, is a claim that the lower text represents an entirely different scripture or removes the Quran's core content.

A third misconception treats the Samarkand and Topkapi codices as literally Uthman's original, blood-marked personal copy. Both are old and significant manuscripts in their own right, but specialists who study their script generally judge them to postdate Uthman's own lifetime, which means the popular attribution to Uthman personally is best understood as a matter of tradition and reverence rather than an established codicological fact.

sections.title: What the Manuscripts Do and Do Not Prove
sections.body:
Taken together, the manuscript evidence establishes something real and specific: within a very short span after Muhammad's death, Quranic text was already being copied onto parchment in a form that reads, for the passages preserved, essentially as it does today. That is a meaningful historical finding, and it fits comfortably alongside the Islamic historical account of an early written compilation under the first caliph Abu Bakr and a standardization under the third caliph Uthman, together with the parallel, uninterrupted chain of memorization that Muslims maintained regardless of which physical copies happened to survive fire, decay, or time.

What the manuscript evidence does not establish, and cannot establish on its own, is the theological claim of Surah Al-Buruj that the Quran's true original is guarded on "a Preserved Slate" (Quran 85:22). That is a claim about God's own guardianship, not a claim that any particular piece of parchment can confirm through a laboratory test. Honest engagement with the manuscripts means holding both truths at once: the physical record is unusually early and stable for a text from this period of history, and it corroborates, without proving outright, the doctrine of preservation that Muslims hold ultimately on grounds of faith.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Is the Birmingham Quran fragment really the oldest Quran manuscript in the world?
A: It is among the oldest known, radiocarbon dated by Oxford to AD 568-645 with 95.4 percent confidence, but dating ranges for a few other early fragments overlap with it. "Among the very oldest known physical fragments" is the more accurate description.

Q: Does the Sanaa palimpsest mean the Quran we have today is wrong?
A: No mainstream scholar studying the manuscript argues that. The scholarly debate concerns how to characterize a variant early textual tradition, not whether today's standard Quran text is a fabrication.

Q: Are the Samarkand and Topkapi codices really Uthman's personal copies?
A: That is the popular tradition surrounding them, but manuscript specialists who study their script generally place their production after Uthman's lifetime, so the attribution is best understood as traditional rather than scientifically confirmed.

Q: How does the Quran's manuscript record compare to the Bible's?
A: The earliest known Quran fragments sit within decades of Muhammad's death, while the earliest surviving complete Bible manuscripts, Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus, were copied roughly three centuries after the events of the Gospels, though the New Testament tradition also includes earlier fragmentary papyri.

Q: Does manuscript evidence prove the Quran is divinely preserved?
A: No single manuscript can. Manuscripts speak only to the human history of copying and transmission. The claim that the Quran's true original is preserved by God, stated in Quran 85:22, is a matter of faith that physical evidence can be consistent with but cannot itself prove.

sections.title: Further Reading
sections.body: The works below go deeper into the manuscripts discussed in this article, from both Islamic and secular academic perspectives.

faq.question: Is the Birmingham Quran fragment really the oldest Quran manuscript in the world?
faq.answer: It is among the oldest known, radiocarbon dated by Oxford to AD 568-645 with 95.4 percent confidence, but dating ranges for a few other early fragments overlap with it, so it is more accurate to call it one of the very oldest known physical fragments rather than definitively the single oldest.

faq.question: Does the Sanaa palimpsest mean the Quran we have today is wrong?
faq.answer: No mainstream scholar studying the manuscript argues that. The scholarly debate concerns how to characterize a variant early textual tradition in the lower text, not whether today's standard Quran text is a fabrication or a different scripture.

faq.question: Are the Samarkand and Topkapi codices really Uthman's personal copies?
faq.answer: That is the popular tradition surrounding them, but manuscript specialists who study their script generally place their production after Uthman's own lifetime, so the attribution to him personally is best understood as traditional and devotional rather than a scientifically confirmed fact.

faq.question: How does the Quran's manuscript record compare to the Bible's?
faq.answer: The earliest known Quran fragments sit within decades of Muhammad's death, while the earliest surviving complete Bible manuscripts, Codex Sinaiticus and Codex Vaticanus, were copied roughly three centuries after the events of the Gospels, though the New Testament tradition also includes earlier fragmentary papyri that predate those complete codices.

faq.question: Does manuscript evidence prove the Quran is divinely preserved?
faq.answer: No single manuscript can prove a theological claim. Manuscripts speak only to the human history of copying and transmission. The claim that the Quran's true original is preserved by God, stated in Quran 85:22, is a matter of faith that the physical evidence can be consistent with but cannot itself prove.

furtherReading.title: The Sanaa Palimpsest: The Transmission of the Qur'an in the First Centuries AH
furtherReading.author: Asma Hilali
furtherReading.note: An Oxford University Press academic study focused specifically on the Sanaa lower text and its significance.

furtherReading.title: Qur'ans of the Umayyads
furtherReading.author: François Déroche
furtherReading.note: A study by a leading specialist in Quranic codicology on how early Quran manuscripts were physically produced.

furtherReading.title: Textual Criticism and Qur'an Manuscripts
furtherReading.author: Keith E. Small
furtherReading.note: A comparative look at variant readings across early Quran manuscripts.

furtherReading.title: Misquoting Jesus
furtherReading.author: Bart D. Ehrman
furtherReading.note: An accessible introduction to New Testament textual criticism from a mainstream biblical scholar, useful for the comparison discussed in this article.
